analysis.note: The compound denotes the immediately preceding threefold consciousness-transformation.

verification.detail: compound stem vijñānapariṇāma as Pum/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Deictic pronoun agreeing with vijñānapariṇāmaḥ/vikalpaḥ; initial a is elided after -o, marked by avagraha.
Predicate nominative: ‘conceptual construction/imagination,’ as glossed by the bhāṣya, not merely ‘alternative/option.’
Relative pronoun, nominative subject of the passive vikalpyate; its referent is the imagined vastu enumerated in the commentary.
Passive: ‘is imagined/conceptually constructed.’ Revised per verifier: the form is derived from the causative/derived verbal base kalpay- with prefix vi, not directly as simple passive vi-√kḷp.
Instrumental referring to vikalpena: ‘by it,’ i.e. by the threefold vikalpa.
Correlative to yat and subject of nāsti: ‘that [imagined object]’.
Negator; in sandhi with asti as nāsti.
Existential verb; in sandhi with na as nāsti.
Adverbial/causal instrumental, explicitly glossed tasmāt by Sthiramati: ‘therefore.’ In external sandhi with idam as tenedaṃ.
Subject-demonstrative in ‘all this’; initial i is absorbed in sandhi after tena.
Appositional/adjectival to idam; the bhāṣya glosses its scope as the three realms and the unconditioned.
Predicate nominative. The -ka is metrical according to the bhāṣya; mātra excludes any object-domain over and above vijñapti.

verification.detail: compound stem vijñāptimātraka as Napumsaka/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation karmadharaya: unsupported (logged)

justifications.decision: vijñānapariṇāmo 'yam is not a generic ‘change of knowledge’; Sthiramati identifies it with the threefold transformation just taught.
justifications.chosen: ‘This transformation of consciousness’ is the immediately preceding threefold vijñānapariṇāma, and it is predicated as vikalpa.

evidence.quote: yo 'yaṃ vijñānapariṇāmas trividho 'nantaram abhihitaḥ so 'yaṃ vikalpaḥ
evidence.translation: ‘This threefold transformation of consciousness that was just stated is this vikalpa.’
‘By that threefold vikalpa, whose nature is storehouse-consciousness, afflicted mind, and active consciousness …’

justifications.decision: The commentary defines vikalpa through adhyāropa and identifies it with abhūtaparikalpa, the false construction constituted by three-realm cittas and caittas.
justifications.chosen: vikalpa is ‘conceptual construction/imagination’ bearing superimposed object-aspects, not ‘option’ or ‘alternative.’

evidence.quote:
adhyāropitārthākārās traidhātukāś cittacaittā vikalpa ucyate
yathoktam
abhūtaparikalpas tu cittacaittās tridhātukā iti
evidence.translation: ‘The cittas and caittas belonging to the three realms, having the aspects of superimposed objects, are called vikalpa; as it has been said: “False construction consists of cittas and caittas of the three realms.”’

justifications.decision: tena is construed instrumentally with vikalpyate, not as the causal ‘therefore’; the relative yad is filled out by Sthiramati as bhājana, ātman, skandhas, dhātus, āyatanas, rūpa, śabda, etc.
justifications.chosen: The first tena means ‘by it,’ i.e. by the threefold vikalpa; yad/tat refers to the imagined object (vastu).

evidence.quote:
tena trividhena vikalpenālayavijñānakliṣṭamanaḥpravṛttivijñānasvabhāvena
sasaṃprayogeṇa yad vikalpyate bhājanam ātmāskandhadhātvāyatanarūpaśabdādikaṃ
vastu tan nāstīty ataḥ sa vijñānapariṇāmo vikalpa ucyate
evidence.translation: ‘By that threefold vikalpa, whose nature is storehouse-consciousness, afflicted mind, and active consciousness, together with its associates, whatever object is imagined—receptacle-world, self, aggregates, elements, sense-bases, form, sound, and so on—that object does not exist; therefore that transformation of consciousness is called vikalpa.’

evidence.quote:
yasmāt pariṇāmātmakena vikalpena yad vikalpyate tena tan
nāsti tasmād viṣayābhāvāt sarvaṃ vijñaptimātrakam
evidence.translation: ‘Because what is imagined by the vikalpa whose nature is transformation—by it, that does not exist; therefore, because of the absence of an object-domain, everything is representation-only.’

justifications.decision: Sthiramati argues for non-existent objects as supports: cognition can arise in illusion, dream, eye-disease, etc.; it arises from a prior same-kind consciousness, not from an external object. Thus the non-existence is targeted at the superimposed object.
options_considered: a blanket nihilistic denial of consciousness and phenomena
denial of the superimposed imagined object as object/support
justifications.chosen: tan nāsti denies the existence of the imagined object/ālambana/viṣaya, not the occurrence of consciousness itself.

evidence.quote:
māyāgandharvanagarasvapnatimirādāv asaty apyālambane jāyate
yadi ca
vijñānasyālambanapratibaddha utpādaḥ syād evaṃ sati māyādiṣv arthābhāvān na
vijñānam utpadyate
tasmāt pūrvakān niruddhāt tajjātīyavijñānād vijñānam utpadyate
na bāhyād
arthāt tasminn asaty api bhāvāt
evidence.translation: ‘Consciousness arises in illusion, a gandharva-city, dream, eye-disease, and the like, even when the object/support is unreal. If the arising of consciousness were bound to an object-support, then in illusions and the like, because there is no object, consciousness would not arise. Therefore consciousness arises from a preceding, ceased consciousness of the same kind, not from an external object, since it exists even when that is absent.’

evidence.quote:
tasmād adhyāropitarūpatvād vikalpasyālambanam asad iti
pratipattavyam
evidence.translation: ‘Therefore, because it has the nature of superimposition, the object/support of vikalpa is to be understood as non-existent.’

justifications.decision: Sthiramati explicitly glosses tena as tasmāt and frames the sentence as avoiding the nihilistic extreme after rejecting superimposition.
justifications.chosen: The second tena, in tenedaṃ, means ‘therefore’ (= tasmāt), leading to vijñaptimātrakam because the object-domain is absent.

evidence.quote:
anena tāvat samāropāntaṃ parihṛtyāpavādāntaparijihīrṣayā āha
tenedaṃ sarvaṃ vijñāptimātrakam
iti
teneti tasmāt
yasmāt pariṇāmātmakena vikalpena yad vikalpyate tena tan
nāsti tasmād viṣayābhāvāt sarvaṃ vijñaptimātrakam
evidence.translation: ‘By this, having first rejected the extreme of superimposition, and wishing to avoid the extreme of denial, he says: “Therefore all this is representation-only.” Here tena means “therefore.” Because what is imagined by the vikalpa whose nature is transformation—by it, that does not exist; therefore, because of the absence of an object-domain, everything is representation-only.’

justifications.decision: The scope of ‘all’ and the force of mātrakam are fixed by explicit glosses: sarvam = traidhātukam plus asaṃskṛtam; mātra excludes what is over and above that, while ka fills the pāda.
options_considered: sarvam only as ordinary conditioned things
sarvam as the three realms and the unconditioned
justifications.chosen: sarvam includes the three realms and the unconditioned; mātra means ‘only’ by excluding any additional object-domain, and -ka is only metrical.

evidence.quote:
sarvam iti traidhātukam
asaṃskṛtañ ca
mātraśabdas tadadhikaviṣayavyavacchedārthaḥ
kakāraḥ
pādapūraṇārthaḥ
evidence.translation: ‘“All” means the three realms and the unconditioned. The word mātra serves to exclude an object-domain over and above that. The syllable ka is for filling out the pāda.’

translation:
This transformation of consciousness (vijñānapariṇāma) is conceptual construction (vikalpa):
whatever object is imagined by it—that is not there.
Therefore all this is representation-only (vijñaptimātraka),
‘all’ meaning the three realms and the unconditioned.
one_shot_delta: A commentary-blind rendering might take vikalpa in its common lexical sense ‘option/alternative’; Sthiramati instead defines it as false conceptual construction with superimposed object-aspects.
It might translate both occurrences of tena the same way. The bhāṣya makes the first instrumental (‘by the threefold vikalpa’) and the second causal (‘therefore’ = tasmāt).
It might read tan nāsti as simple nihilism. Sthiramati restricts the denial to the imagined object/support and then explicitly frames vijñaptimātra as avoiding the extreme of denial.
It might leave sarvam vague or restrict it to conditioned three-realm experience; the commentary includes both the three realms and the unconditioned.
It might treat -mātrakam as a substantive ‘measure’ or make -ka meaningful; Sthiramati says mātra excludes an additional object-domain and ka is only pādapūraṇa.
analyzer_disagreements: ByT5 prints the compound members vijñāna + pariṇāmaḥ and vijñāpti + mātrakam separately; per the required format I give the inflected compounds under the whole stems vijñānapariṇāma and vijñāptimātraka.

ByT5 leaves the two occurrences of tena undifferentiated; the commentary distinguishes the first as instrumental ‘by the threefold vikalpa’ (trbh 1100–1101) and the second as causal ‘therefore’ = tasmāt (trbh 1124–1126).
